title: NDM troubleshooting
description: NDM platform health: agents, SNMP reachability and poll cost, traps, NetFlow pipeline internals, ICMP and SNMP integration memory. Pair with Interface Performance (same snmp_device/interface) for per-interface bit rates, errors, and state.

definition.content:
**Scope here:** Agent health, SNMP check cost, autodiscovery, traps, and NetFlow **pipeline** metrics (receive, flush, index). **Per-interface** utilization, errors, packet mix, and detailed state history live on [Interface Performance](/dash/integration/Interface%20Performance?live=true&tpl_var_snmp_host={{snmp_host.value}}&tpl_var_snmp_device={{snmp_device.value}}&tpl_var_interface={{interface.value}})—set **snmp_device** + **interface** there to match the device you care about here (`$SNMP_Device` is the same device tag as **snmp_device** on that dashboard).

**How the panels fit together:** Agent availability, SNMP reachability and poll duration, trap flow, then NetFlow received/flushed/indexed; version-specific sequence/drop widgets sit with the pipeline section. **At the bottom:** SNMP integration memory and ICMP latency/unreachable help separate agent or path issues from the device’s SNMP behavior.

**Filters:** `$Agent_Hostname`, `$SNMP_Device`, `$Netflow_Exporter`, `$Custom_Tag`. [NDM docs](https://docs.datadoghq.com/network_monitoring/devices/).

**Why are my devices unreachable?**

An unreachable device means that the agent cannot collect metrics from it.
Reachability is determined by performing a GETNEXT on the OID '1.0'. 
-  Try loosening ACLs/firewall rules for your devices. To check connectivity from the Agent host to a network device, use the integrated `agent snmp walk` command.

**Are you using the Python integration ?**

Network Device Monitoring requires the SNMP core check, which is substantially faster and includes the latest features.

 To migrate from the Python integration to the core check, see [Migrating to the SNMP Core Check](https://docs.datadoghq.com/network_monitoring/devices/guide/migrating-to-snmp-core-check).

definition.title: Devices monitored using the Python integration

definition.content:
**Why are my devices matched to the generic device profile?**

Some devices may not be supported out-of-the-box and default to the generic device profile, which may cause some missing metrics.

 To create a custom SNMP profile for your network devices, see [Build a NDM Profile](https://docs.datadoghq.com/network_monitoring/devices/guide/build-ndm-profile/).

**Abnormal check duration/interval?**

- Try increasing the number of check runners (`check_runners:`) in your `datadog.yaml` configuration.
- Try also increasing the `oid_batch_size` in your SNMP configuration. **Note:** A high value can lead to timeouts on some network devices.
- If neither suggestions work, you may need to split the monitoring of your devices across several agents.

**Why are no metrics being collected?**

- Try loosening ACLs/firewall rules for your devices. You can check connectivity from the Agent host to a network device by using the integrated `agent snmp walk` command.
- Check connectivity between the Agent host and Datadog.

You can find more information on how to setup network devices Autodiscovery using the Datadog Agent. 

 See [Collect SNMP Metrics From Your Network Devices](https://docs.datadoghq.com/network_monitoring/devices/snmp_metrics/?tab=snmpv2#autodiscovery).

definition.title: Traps received, forwarded and stored in Datadog

definition.content:
**Why are traps not being received?**

- Check your device configuration and ensure it is sending traps to the right port.
- Check the Traps server listening port and ensure it is using an unprivileged port (> 1024). Or, allow the Datadog Agent to listen on privileged ports.

Traps telemetry metrics requires Agent 7.47+

definition.title: Netflow records received, flushed, and stored in Datadog

formulas.alias: Flows sent by agent to backend

definition.content:
**Why is there a difference between the number of received Netflow records and Netflow records sent to Datadog?**

Netflow records are aggregated Agent side, with a default flush time of 5 minutes. It means that if two records with the same source, destination, and protocol are collected within 5 minutes, they appear as one in the UI.

definition.title: Gap in Flows (Agent Flushed - Stored in Datadog)
NetFlow records indexed by Exporter IP

definition.content:
**What is NetFlow port rollup?**

NetFlow records with a source or destination port that is detected as ephemeral are aggregated together. To detect these ephemeral ports, Datadog keeps a record of every source/destination port combination seen in the last 5 minutes. If a specific source port is associated with more than 10 different destination ports during that time, the destination port is marked as ephemeral and all NetFlow records with this source port will be aggregated.

**Why are packets not being received?**

- Check your NetFlow exporter configuration and ensure it is sending flows to the right port.
- Check the NetFlow server listening port and ensure that it is using an unprivileged port (> 1024). Or, allow the Datadog Agent to listen on privileged ports.

**Why are there bursts in buffer length?**

If the buffer length is close to the buffer capacity, you can increase the buffer capacity by updating `aggregator_buffer_size` in your `datadog.yaml` configuration.
The following widget is only valid for **NetFlow5/SFlow**.
The following widget is only valid for **NetFlow9/IPFIX**.

**How can I detect missing NetFlow records?**

NetFlow missing packet detection helps you identify potential issues in your network. It is an estimation of the number of packet (or flows, depending on NetFlow version) that are lost.

This metric can appear noisy depending on time aggregation, but a significant increase indicates that some NetFlow packets are lost between the exporter and the Datadog Agent.

definition.title: SNMP agent memory and ICMP reachability

definition.content: **Agent-side SNMP & ICMP:** High `datadog.snmp.memory.used` can correlate with large MIB walks. `networkdevice.ping.*` complements SNMP reachability for dual-stack checks.
